title: Is There a Risk Premium in the Stock Lending Market? Evidence from Equity Options
abstract: Recent research argues that uncertainty about future stock borrowing fees hinders short-selling, and this risk explains the performance of short strategies. One possible mechanism is that borrowing fee risk carries a risk premium. Since the present value of the uncertain borrowing fee is reflected in options prices, the difference between option-implied and realized fees estimates this premium. We find that the risk premium is small. Moreover, if the risk premium is substantial, it should be reflected in the returns to short-selling stock after adjusting for stock borrowing fees. However, borrowing fee risk does not predict fee-adjusted returns.

article.introduction:
A large literature shows that proxiesfor short-selling activity and short-sale constraints, including short interest and stock borrowing fees among other variables, predict the cross section of stock returns.1The magnitude and persistence of this predictability, however, remain a puzzle. One possible explanation is suggested by the market practice whereby stock loans may be recalled at any time, forcing market participants to either cover their short positions and return the borrowed stock or reestablish their stock loans at a possibly much higher borrowing fee, making the borrowing fee for a period longer than a day uncertain. Indeed, D'Avolio (2002, p. 279) emphasizes that “…a short seller is concerned not only with the level of fees, but also with fee variance.” More recently, Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) argue that the risk of a borrowing fee increase is an important impediment to short positions and provide evidence consistent with the view that short fee risk predicts stock returns.
An implication of the Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) analysis is that short sellers should receive a risk premium for exposure to the risks of stock loan recalls and borrowing fee changes. If there were a market for term stock borrowing, the borrowing fee risk premium could be measured by the difference between the term borrowing fee and the expectation of the accumulated overnight fee over the life of the term loan. However, there is no active market for term stock loans.2Instead, the term borrowing fee can be inferred or “implied” from options prices. This option-implied term borrowing fee should reflect a risk premium according to Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018). This premium can be estimated as the average difference between the option-implied fee and the accumulated borrowing fee during the lives of the put-call option pair used to compute the option-implied fee. A second implication of Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) is that the returns to short-selling net of the stock borrowing fees that must be paid to maintain the short position should also reflect a risk premium. That is, the typical net-of-fee return on stock positions exposed to short fee risk should be substantially higher than the net-of-fee return on otherwise identical positions not exposed to short fee risk. Thus, short fee risk should predict the net-of-fee return to shorting. Moreover, stock returns before adjusting for fees should be predictable using short fee risk after controlling for other measures of short-sale constraints. We test these predictions and find that the evidence does not support them.
Ofek, Richardson, and Whitelaw (2004) and Evans et al. (2009) provide evidence that deviations from the textbook put-call parity relation are correlated with stock borrowing costs. This result suggests that one can infer stock borrowing costs from option prices. We begin our analysis by showing that stock borrowing fees are related to option prices through a version of the put-call parity relation augmented to include the borrowing fee. Omitting this fee leads to apparent violations of the textbook put-call parity relation that does not account for the fee. The option-implied borrowing fee can be computed from options prices using the version of the put-call parity relation that we derive. The option-implied fee is the risk-adjusted expectation of borrowing fee payments during the remaining life of the put-call pair of options used to compute the fee; it reflects both the expected borrowing cost over the lives of the options used and any risk premium for bearing the risk of borrowing fee changes. We also show that exposure to borrowing fee risk might command a risk premium through its possible correlation with the stochastic discount factor in a standard asset pricing model.
Next, we discuss the main results on the risk premium for bearing the risk of changes in the borrowing fee. The average difference between the option-implied borrowing fee, calculated using end-of-day quotes from OptionMetrics and the Center for Research in Securities Prices (CRSP), and the accumulated borrowing fee during the options’ lives from Markit provides an estimate of the borrowing fee risk premium. We compute this difference each trading day for a large set of optionable stocks and use the average difference to estimate the risk premium. This is analogous to the approach in the interest rate literature whereby the difference between the three-month interest rate and the average overnight rate during the three-month term provides an estimate of the risk premium for bearing interest rate risk. A similar idea appears in the literature on the variance risk premium, where the variance risk premium is the average difference between model-free option-implied variance and realized stock return variance (Carr and Wu (2009)).
If the borrowing fee is constant or has very low volatility, this risk premium should be nonexistent or small. However, if the borrowing fee is volatile, options market participants may be compensated for bearing this risk. Of course, a volatile borrowing fee is a necessary, but not sufficient, condition for a risk premium in typical asset pricing models. The borrowing fee must also contain components that covary with the stochastic discount factor of at least some market participants to generate a risk premium.
On average, the option-implied borrowing fees are similar to the accumulated indicative borrowing costs during the lives of the options from which the option-implied fees were computed. Indeed, the mean (median) estimate of the risk premium is only 31 (28) basis points (bps) per year. For hard-to-borrow stocks, that is, stocks with borrowing fees greater than 1%, the mean (median) estimate of the risk premium is 19 (23) bps per year. Thus, our results show that this risk premium is typically small, even for stocks that are hard to borrow. An estimated risk premium near zero indicates that the risk-adjusted performance of about 1% per month for several shorting strategies is not explained by compensation for exposure to borrowing fee risk.
Our calculations use midpoint option prices. One potential concern with this analysis is that options’ bid-ask midpoints are not equal to the options’ unobserved fair values as determined by the stochastic discount factor of the marginal market participant. We address this concern by assuming that the borrowing fee based on the options’ unobserved fair values must lie between the minimum and maximum fees computed from option bid and ask prices. For each stock-day, we select the put-call pair with the tightest spread between minimum and maximum implied fees and estimate the implied borrowing fee and the related risk premium using this put-call pair. Based on this approach, we estimate the risk premium for a series of subsamples with successively tighter restrictions on the difference between the maximum and minimum option-implied borrowing fees. The risk premium is close to zero in each of these subsamples.
We also examine other subsamples that focus on specific time periods of interest, to analyze alternative explanations, or that restrict observations so that the implied fee is measured using options with particular characteristics: options with low bid-ask spreads, options on high-priced stocks, options on high-volatility stocks, and options on stocks with high options trading volume. With only one notable exception discussed below, the results in these subsamples are similar to those for the full sample and indicate that the risk premium is close to zero.
We repeat the main analysis using synchronized intraday bid and ask quotes at a one-minute frequency for U.S. listed options and their underlying equities. This analysis results in small estimates of the risk premium that are consistent with those obtained using end-of-day option price quotes.
The borrowing fee risk premium is close to zero except during the financial crisis, which we define as July 2008 to June 2009. During this period, the average risk premium is 4.02% per year for the hard-to-borrow subsample. This estimate is much larger than the estimate of the risk premium for the full sample of 0.31%. We therefore find a significant risk premium for the hard-to-borrow stocks during the crisis. The premium for stocks that are not hard to borrow remains small. We also find evidence consistent with increased risk premiums for the stocks affected by the 2008 ban on short sales of financial stocks immediately before and after the ban during the financial crisis. During these periods, market participants were plausibly worried about the possible imposition or reimposition of a ban. The estimated borrowing fee risk premium for affected stocks is about 1% larger than the estimated risk premium to borrow comparable nonfinancial stocks around the ban. This difference is statistically significant.3
The two findings above can be interpreted in two ways. First, the period from July 2008 through June 2009 and the period around the short-sale ban were both unusual and relatively brief. Perhaps the average realized indicative borrowing fees during these periods were less than expected and thus the realized risk premium exceeded its expectation. Alternatively, many aspects of financial intermediation became more exposed to systematic risk during these periods, resulting in volatile borrowing fees that carry a risk premium. Under the latter interpretation, the results above help validate our estimates of the risk premium because we find evidence of a risk premium in the two situations for which a risk premium is most likely. Regardless, the apparent increase in the risk premium is limited to the financial crisis period and thus cannot explain the abnormal performance of short strategies documented in the literature.
Turning to the second implication of Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg's (2018) analysis, we examine whether short fee risk predicts the net-of-fee returns to shorting. Because short sellers must pay the borrowing fee, the net return that they receive is the return on the stock less the borrowing fee they pay, that is, the net-of-fee return. If there is a risk premium for bearing short fee risk, short fee risk should predict the net-of-fee return. When we use univariate predictive panel regressions, we show that short fee risk predicts theunadjustedreturns to shorting in our sample, consistent with the main findings in Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018). However, when we replace the unadjusted returns with net-of-fee returns, short fee risk does not predict net-of-fee returns either in the full sample or in a subsample of hard-to-borrow stocks. In both cases, the point estimates of the coefficients on short fee risk are close to zero and thet-statistics are small. These results are inconsistent with one of the central implications of Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018).
We further show that short fee risk does not predict the unadjusted returns to shorting when we include additional covariates in the predictive panel regressions, such as the option-implied fee, the indicative borrowing fee, short interest, log market capitalization, and utilization. In the full sample, the coefficients on short fee risk are insignificant while those on the option-implied borrowing fee and some of the other covariates are significant. In the hard-to-borrow subsample, the option-implied borrowing fee is the only significant return predictor. Thus, short fee risk is not a robust predictor of stock returns when other proxy variables for short-sale constraints and short-selling activity are included in the regression.
We next validate our computation and interpretation of the fee. The risk premium should be close to zero for (i) stocks that are easy to borrow, and especially, (ii) stocks that are easy to borrow and also have low short fee risk. We find that risk premiums are close to zero in these two subsamples. These results validate our approach in general and support our specific choices regarding, for example, the way we address the possible early exercise of options. If our various computation decisions were not appropriate, we would not expect to recover near-zero risk premiums for these subsamples. Also, the results show that options’ large bid-ask spreads do not prevent accurate estimation of the risk premium. Moreover, in panel regressions we show that the option-implied fee, together with the indicative fee, predicts future changes in the indicative borrowing fee. This result is robust to including short fee risk, short interest, log market capitalization, and utilization in the regressions. Thus, the option-implied fee contains information about current and expected future borrowing costs.
Ofek, Richardson, and Whitelaw (2004) is an early paper that shows deviations from the textbook put-call parity relation are correlated with stock lending fees and predict stock returns. This finding is interpreted as evidence that the equities and options markets are segmented due to short-sale costs, with the equities market being “less rational” than the options market (Ofek, Richardson, and Whitelaw (2004, p. 307)). In contrast, Evans et al. (2009) view the stock and options markets as connected through the market for borrowing shares and provide evidence that deviations from the textbook put-call parity relation are related to options market makers’ stock borrowing costs, including the cost reductions arising from options market makers’ ability to short-sell without first borrowing shares. We also view the stock and options markets as connected through the market for stock lending and interpret deviations from the textbook put-call parity relation using an augmented put-call parity relation that includes the fee to borrow stock. In effect, the disparity measure of Evans et al. (2009) must be transformed using the nonlinear equation provided in SectionIand the specific parameters of the option pair in the put-call parity relation to recover the implied fee. We show that this option-implied stock borrowing fee predicts stock returns while controlling for other measures of short-selling activity. Muravyev, Pearson, Pollet (2022) provide evidence that the implied volatility spread (Cremers and Weinbaum (2010)) and implied volatility skew (Xing, Zhang, and Zhao (2010)) predict returns because they proxy for the stock borrowing fee. Weitzner (2020) also derives option-implied stock borrowing fees and provides evidence of stock return predictability.
Our result that option-implied fees predict stock returns is also closely related to the literature that uses stock borrowing fees to predict returns. Drechsler and Drechsler (2016) show that borrowing fees predict stock returns and explain the returns to shorting as a premium for bearing the risk that is correlated with the returns to short sellers’ (concentrated) portfolios. Our findings are consistent with this hypothesis. Geczy, Musto, and Reed (2002), Ofek, Richardson, and Whitelaw (2004), and Cohen, Diether, and Malloy (2009) also present evidence that fees negatively predict stock returns.
While we obtain our results for a sample of optionable stocks rather than all stocks, our sample includes on average 1,625 stocks each month. This sample comprises 92% of the market capitalization of stocks with indicative borrowing fee data available in Markit. The nonoptionable stocks not in our sample tend to be small and illiquid and thus are of limited interest to institutional investors.
The implied fee can be used as a market-based substitute for the indicative borrowing fee when the latter variable is not available. This approach may be particularly useful for analyses of long-short trading strategies before 2007. For example, option price data are available between 1996 and 2006, but Markit did not systematically collect stock borrowing fee data during this period. Practitioners may also find that option-implied fees are useful estimates of expected future borrowing fees.
The balance of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIintroduces the option-implied borrowing fee and describes how we compute it. SectionIIdescribes the stock borrowing fee and option price data. SectionIIIpresents estimates of the borrowing fee risk premium. SectionIVexamines whether short fee risk predicts both unadjusted returns and fee-adjusted returns. SectionVdescribes results of several additional analyses that validate our computation and interpretation of option-implied borrowing fees. SectionVIbriefly concludes.
article.I. Derivation of Option-Implied Fees and:
In this section, we analyze how the stock borrowing fees that a short seller must pay to borrow shares of a stock are related to option prices. For simplicity, we initially assume that options are of the European type, the interest rate is constant, and the stock does not pay a dividend before option expiration. The stochastic discount factor of a representative agent determines the risk-adjusted present value of the daily borrowing fee. We also assume that the stock lender does not require that the stock borrower overcollateralize the stock loan.4
Consider a discrete-time setting with risky borrowing payments that are made once each day from timetto the expiration of the put and call pair at timeT=t+k. The variablesStandhtare the stock price and borrowing fee at datet. An investor who buys the stock at datetat priceStcan lend the stock and receivehtStat timet+ 1. Note that similar to an overnight interest rate,htis quoted at timetbuthtStis paid at the end of the period at timet+ 1. LetR∼ht,t+jequal (St+j/St) – 1, which does not include the claim to borrowing fee payments. We defineRt,t+jfor the same length of time as the return including borrowing fee payments; this is the return received by the representative investor that determines stock prices and the stochastic discount factor. Of course, the risk of changes in the borrowing fee might be priced because the fee might covary with the stochastic discount factor. LetMt+jbe the stochastic discount factor from periodtto periodt+jand letVt+jbe the risk-adjusted present value ofht+j−1St+j−1, which is the borrowing fee paid at datet+jbased onht+j−1quoted att+j−1. Also letrbe the one-day interest rate and letδ= 1/(1 +r) be the one-day discount factor.
Note thatEt[Mt+j−1St+j−1] is the present value of the underlying stock received at datet+j− 1 without any claim to borrowing fees (or dividends) beforet+j− 1. If the borrowing fee is not included in the return to the representative investor, thenEt[Mt+j−1St+j−1] =St. However, we assume that the representative investor receives the borrowing fee, so the present value of the future stock price is lower than the current stock price. This situation is similar to the presence of a dividend payment.
The term (1 −δhQ)j−1adjusts the present value of the future stock price for the borrowing fee payments that are not received by the representative investor. Note that for a small number of time periods and/or smallhQ, the right-hand side of equation (5) is approximatelykδhQSt, where this approximation is exact if the borrowing fee is not included in the return received by the representative investor.
If dateTis an option's expiration date, then the constant risk-adjusted feehQon the left-hand side can be computed from option prices. The average borrowing fee can be computed from realized borrowing fees over the life of the option. The risk premium can then be estimated as the average of the difference betweenhQand the average borrowing fee during the life of the option. This risk premium is due to the covariance of the borrowing fee with the product of the stochastic discount factor and the gross stock return without the claim to the fee. Our approach is similar to estimating the variance risk premium as the difference between implied and future realized variances, as in Carr and Wu (2009).
This computation of the option-implied borrowing fee accounts for the discrete nature of borrowing fee payments and generates implied fees similar to an alternative simplified approach where the borrowing fee is a continuous flow analogous to the continuous dividend yield that appears in some versions of the Black-Scholes-Merton formula.
Turning back to the risk premium in equation (9), we can replace the first term on the left-hand side with the option-implied borrowing fee based on options expiring at timeT. We replace the second term on the left-hand side, the expected average of daily borrowing fees, with the actual average of the daily borrowing fees fromttoT. The difference on the left-hand side should be positive if the product of the stochastic discount factor and gross stock return covaries with the borrowing fee to a significant extent. Of course, it is also possible that this covariance is near zero because the realized borrowing fee is largely idiosyncratic. Indeed, we have verified that the daily borrowing fee appears to be largely uncorrelated with the contemporaneous stock return as well as the next day's stock return.
Actual equity options have American-, not European-, style exercise, and often their underlying stocks pay dividends. We minimize the impact of biases created by possible early exercise of American options and our use of the European put-call parity relation by excluding from the sample in-the-money puts and options whose underlying stocks pay large dividends during the lives of the options. We also restrict attention to options with between 15 and 90 calendar days to expiration because options with shorter maturities usually have large relative bid-ask spreads and options with longer maturities trade less frequently.
These sample restrictions do not address the fact that, when the borrowing fee is large, the fee itself can make early exercise of an American call optimal even when the stock does not pay any dividends during the life of the option. This impacts the value of an American call option, and hence the option-implied borrowing fees that we compute using the European put-call parity relation are downward-biased. Jensen and Pedersen (2016) show that high borrowing fees explain the otherwise surprising exercise of deep-in-the-money calls on stocks. We assess the magnitude of the bias by using the binomial model to compute American option prices for a grid of borrowing fees and option volatilities using plausible values for the moneyness, time-to-expiration, and risk-free rate. For each put-call pair, we estimate the difference between the true borrowing fee and the option-implied fee computed using equation (15). Repeating this procedure for many put-call pairs, we use a regression model to estimate how the difference betweeen the true fees and option-implied fees depends on the option parameters.
We find that the bias due to the use of the European put-call parity relation can be important when the borrowing fee is large and the moneyness (K/S− 1) is small so that call options are well in-the-money. The regression analysis shows that the bias is not sensitive to the option volatility and is well explained by a linear function of the term min(h× (K/S −1), 0), wherehis the indicative borrowing fee from Markit. We compute this linear function using the regression estimates and add the adjustment to the option-implied borrowing fees computed using the European put-call parity relation. We use the resulting adjusted option-implied borrowing fees to estimate the risk premium using equation (9). However, we use the unadjusted option-implied fees to predict stock returns because the adjustment uses the indicative fee. Due to this potential for contamination, using the adjusted fees in predictive regressions would make it difficult to conclude whether the predictive ability was due to the option-implied fee or the indicative fee component of the adjustment for early exercise.
article.II. Data and Summary Statistics:
We use stock borrowing fees and other data on stock borrowing and lending activity from the Markit Securities Finance Buy Side Analytics Data Feed available from Markit, Ltd. The Markit database includes daily data on securities borrowing activity, including rebates and loan fees, the quantity on loan, the number of loans, the numbers of active brokers, and lending agents. Markit obtains this information from more than 100 equity loan market participants, including beneficial owners, hedge funds, investment banks, lending agents, and prime brokers, who together account for approximately 85% of U.S. securities loans (Markit (2012)). While the Markit data set includes a broader range of securities, the analyses are limited to the subset of U.S. equities that have exchange-traded options. Our sample begins in July 2006 because data coverage expanded significantly around that time and the data are available at a daily frequency beginning June 28, 2006. The sample ends in August 2015.
The market for borrowing stock is described in other papers, including D'Avolio (2002) and Kolasinski, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2013). To summarize, this market includes three groups of participants: (i) lenders such as mutual funds, pension funds, and insurance companies, some of which lend through agent lenders (custodians), (ii) ultimate borrowers, for example, hedge funds, proprietary trading desks, and options market makers, and (iii) prime brokers. Typically hedge funds and options market makers borrow securities from their prime brokers, who in turn borrow from mutual funds, pension funds, and other ultimate lenders (Kolasinski, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2013), especially Figure1). The prime brokers “mark up” the fee, that is, they borrow from the original lender and then relend to the hedge fund, options market maker, or other short seller at a higher fee. According to D'Avolio (2002), the ratio of lender-side fees to borrower-side fees is approximately 70%.
Borrowing fees typically are not quoted directly but rather are derived from quoted rebate rates. The security borrower usually provides cash collateral to the security lender, and the security lender pays interest (the rebate rate) on the cash collateral that it holds. The borrowing fee is the difference between the market short-term interest rate and the rebate rate paid on the cash collateral.5The rebate rate can be negative when securities are hard to borrow and the borrowing fee is high. The borrowing fee can also be negative, which occurs when the rebate rate that the security lender pays on cash collateral exceeds the short-term interest rate.
The market structure in which prime brokers are financial intermediaries implies that there are two fees, a buy-side fee paid by the ultimate borrower (for example, a hedge fund or an options market maker) and a lender-side fee received by the ultimate lender (for example, a mutual fund). The main borrowing fee variable that we use is “IndicativeFee,” which is a buy-side fee. Specifically, this variable is Markit's estimate of the “The expected borrow cost, in fee terms, for a hedge fund on a given day,” based on “both borrow costs between Agent Lenders and Prime Brokers as well as rates from hedge funds to produce an indication of the current market rate” (Markit (2012)).6We use the indicative fees on the reported day. We verify that using a settlement lag of three days for the fees as in Richardson, Saffi, and Sigurdsson (2017) and Saffi and Sigurdsson (2011), as is common in the security lending market, does not change any of our results.
The Markit buy-side data also include the quantity on loan (QuantityOnLoan) and the utilization rate (Utilization), defined as the ratio of the quantity on loan to the lendable quantity (LendableQuantity), which we use in some analyses. In addition to the Markit data, we use end-of-day option price quotes from OptionMetrics. Stock prices, returns, and dividend information (amounts and ex-dividend dates) come from the files maintained by CRSP, and we address NASDAQ delisting returns as in Shumway and Warther (1999). Our analyses use interest rates that we infer from the prices of liquid options for stocks that are extremely easy to borrow. While we think this approach provides a more accurate estimate of borrowing costs for options market makers, the main findings do not change if we use the relevant LIBOR rates instead.
We also use intraday bid and ask option and equity quotes obtained from Nanex, a firm specializing in high-quality data feeds, to replicate the main risk premium results. Nanex obtains its data from standard sources: the Option Price Reporting Authority (OPRA) for options and the Securities Information Processor (SIP) for equities. The Nanex data consist of all trades and intraday bid and ask quotes at a one-minute frequency for all U.S. listed equity options and their underlying equities, with the restriction that the quotes for an option series are not included for dates when the series does not trade.7This restriction reduces the number of stock-date observations relative to the OptionMetrics sample. It also reduces the number of put-call pairs used to compute the option-implied fees for the stock-dates that appear in the Nanex sample. For these reasons, and to allow for replication by other researchers, we use daily data from CRSP and OptionMetrics for most of the analyses reported in this paper.
The sample only includes a stock on a particular date if the Markit indicative fee is available on that date, market capitalization is greater than or equal to $10 million, and the stock price is greater than or equal to $5 per share. In addition, the stock must be optionable and there must be at least one put-call pair that satisfies the following criteria: (i) the put moneynessK/Sis less than or equal to 1.1, (ii) the number of days remaining to expiration is between 15 and 90 days, (iii) the sum of the call and put bid-ask spreads divided by the stock price is less than or equal to 5%, and (iv) the sum of dividends paid during the remaining life of the option is less than 5% of the stock price. In addition, for each option in a put-call pair, open interest is positive, the absolute delta is between 0.01 and 0.99, implied volatility is between 0.03 and 2, the bid is greater than 0.1, and the bid is less than the ask.8Our main results are similar if any one of these restrictions is dropped. After applying these filters, we compute an option-implied borrowing fee from put-call pairs as described in SectionIIon at least one day during the month for an average of 1,625 stocks per month. The combined market capitalization of the stocks with an implied fee is 92% of the total market capitalization of the stocks covered by Markit for the average month.
TableIreports various percentiles of the distributions of the Markit stock borrowing fee and rebate data, and also of several variables we compute from the Markit data, for the set of stock-dates for which a common stock appears in CRSP, an indicative fee is available in the Markit data, and an option-implied borrowing fee can be computed. The first row of the table reveals that the mean indicative fee is 0.97% per year and that the indicative fee is positively skewed. The indicative fee is 0.25% at the 1stpercentile, 0.375% at the 10thand 50thpercentiles, 0.625% at the 90thpercentile, and 15% at the 99thpercentile. In the next row, the rebate is negatively skewed, with values of −13.87% at the 1stpercentile (note that large negative rebates correspond to large positive fees), −0.42% at the 10thpercentile, and −0.22% at the 50thpercentile. The mean value of the rebate is −0.29%.
For each stock-date, we use the median option-implied fee across the put-call pairs on that date as the estimate of the option-implied fee. Using the mean instead of the median produces similar results. The row labeled “Option-implied fee, unadj.” reports summary statistics for the estimate of the option-implied fee without any adjustment for the bias caused by applying a version of the European put-call parity relation to American option prices. The row labeled “Option-implied fee, adjusted” includes an adjustment to remove this bias, as described in SectionI.
The mean and median of the adjusted option-implied fee, 1.27% and 0.77%, are somewhat larger than the mean and median of the indicative borrowing fee reported in the first row of TableI. However, the distribution of the option-implied fee exhibits much more dispersion than the distribution of the indicative fee the 90thpercentile is 4.73% and the 10thpercentile is actually negative at −2.14%. The statistics for the unadjusted option-implied fees are similar, although the mean and all percentiles are somewhat smaller, reflecting the adjustment for early exercise. The dispersion in the option-implied fees reflects the fact that implied fees are estimated with error, some of which is microstructure “noise” stemming from the wide bid-ask spreads in the option market. In general, this problem should make it more difficult to find evidence that the option-implied fee predicts stock returns because these measurement errors likely cause estimates of the coefficients on the option-implied fee to be biased toward zero as a result of the classic errors-in-variables bias.
The next two rows report statistics for utilization and short interest, both of which are also right-skewed. The mean utilization is 20.25% compared to a median of 12.27%, and the 90thand 99thpercentiles are 53.09% and 85.58%, respectively. Short interest is computed from the Markit data and is defined as the quantity on loan divided by shares outstanding (from CRSP). After short interest, we present statistics for the short fee risk measure introduced by Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2015). This variable is defined as the natural log of the variance of the daily indicative fee over the preceding 12 months and has mean and median values of 3.41 and 3.66, respectively.9
The last two rows provide some information about the market capitalization of the stocks in our sample. The typical stock is in the seventh NYSE size decile, with mean and median market capitalization of $28,872 and $2,440 million, respectively. Unsurprisingly, the sample is tilted toward larger stocks in CRSP due to the limitation to optionable stocks and the filters based on stock and option characteristics. This sample implicitly excludes microcap stocks that are often a source of statistically significant asset pricing anomalies.
TableIIreports the correlation matrix of selected characteristics for the sample used in the paper. The correlation between the unadjusted option-implied fee and the indicative fee is 0.59. The correlation between the adjusted option-implied fee and the indicative fee is higher, at 0.69. Estimation errors due to options’ wide bid-ask spreads may be an important reason these correlations are less than one. However, because the main results about the estimated risk premium are based on averages, the errors in estimating the implied fee will bias the main results only if the mean error is substantially different from zero. Moreover, estimation error is not the only explanation for deviations between the implied fee and the current indicative fee. The risk-adjusted expected fee during the remaining life of the put-call pair may be different from the current indicative fee because the borrowing fee is expected to change.
The fee measures are not as highly correlated with short interest, a publicly available proxy for short-sale constraints that is widely used in the literature, or short fee risk. For instance, the correlation between the indicative fee and short fee risk (short interest) is only 0.29 (0.24). Similarly, the correlation between the unadjusted option-implied fee and short fee risk (short interest) is only 0.22 (0.22). The correlation between each of the different borrowing fee measures and market capitalization is near zero.
One clear benefit of our approach is the estimation of stock borrowing costs during the period before Markit began collecting borrowing fee data. In particular, option-implied borrowing fees can be a useful proxy for the actual borrowing fees faced by the marginal investor during historical periods in which indicative fees are not available.
article.III. Estimating the Borrowing Fee Risk P:
We use option price bid-ask midpoints to compute the option-implied borrowing fee via equation (15). By using the midpoints, we are assuming that options’ bid-ask midpoints are (possibly noisy) proxies for their fair market values. Our main findings are based on averages, and we have no reason to expect bid-ask midpoints to be systematically above or below options fair values. Thus, we do not expect the fact that midpoints are noisy proxies for fair values to have an important impact on our results. Nevertheless, we address this potential concern in TableVby using subsamples with increasingly tighter restrictions on the bid-ask spreads of the options used to compute the option-implied fees to examine whether the results continue to hold when possible errors in estimating the option-implied fee are smaller.
We begin our analysis of the borrowing fee risk premium by using two stocks to illustrate the direct link between the implied borrowing fee and the indicative fee. Panel A of Figure1presents the daily time series of the option-implied and indicative borrowing fees for Tesla, Inc. starting from shortly after its initial public offering. The panel shows that the option-implied and indicative fees track each other closely throughout the period despite the wide options bid-ask spreads, with the two fees moving together through several large changes in their levels. The relatively small differences in the two fee series appear to be random “noise” that may stem from the fact that option bid-ask midpoints are imperfect proxies for the options’ fair values. The formula for the option-implied fee (equation (15)) involves both call and put prices, divided by the stock price. Thus, the sum of the call and put bid-ask spreads divided by the stock price is a relevant measure of the options bid-ask spread. This measure, averaged across the put-call pairs used to compute the Tesla implied fee for each date and then averaged across dates, is 1.23% of the Tesla stock price, which averaged $106.40 per share. It is 2.22% of the stock price for the 2010 to 2011 period when the average stock price was $25.71 per share.
Panel B shows the daily indicative and the option-implied borrowing fee for Sears Holdings Corporation. Again, the option-implied and indicative fees are tightly linked, with the peaks and valleys in the time path of the indicative fee being tracked closely by the implied fee. During this period, the sum of the put and call bid-ask spreads averaged 1.09% of the stock price ($77.77 per share on average). The spread was higher during 2014 to 2015 after a decline in Sears’ price in early 2014. These patterns in both panels in Figure1illustrate that the option-implied borrowing fee is closely related to the indicative borrowing fee. TheInternet Appendixincludes corresponding figures for two more stocks.10
TableIIIprovides evidence about the magnitude of the borrowing fee risk premium by reporting the mean and median of the risk premium, defined as the difference between the option-implied fee and the realized indicative borrowing fee, for the full sample and a subsample of stock-dates for which the underlying stock was hard to borrow. For each stock-date, we compute the option-implied fee for each put-call pair and then take the median across the option pairs for the stock. We also adjust each of the option-implied fees for the early exercise premium as described above. For each option, we compute the average indicative borrowing fee over the remaining life of the option. The realized risk premium is the difference between the option-implied borrowing fee and this average indicative borrowing fee.
In the first row, the borrowing fee risk premium (adjusted) reports the mean, median, and standard deviation of the realized risk premium for the full sample and a subsample of hard-to-borrow stocks. This estimate of the risk premium is based on the adjusted option-implied fee. Thet-statistic for the mean risk premium is in the next row. The table also reports the mean and median values of the risk premium computed from option-implied borrowing fees that are not adjusted for early exercise, the adjusted and unadjusted option-implied borrowing fees, where the option-implied borrowing fees are computed as described above, and the indicative borrowing fee. The estimates in this table indicate that the risk premium in the stock borrowing market is relatively modest. In the full sample, the mean and median risk premia estimates are 0.31% and 0.28% per year, respectively. While thet-statistic of 5.0 indicates that the estimate of the mean adjusted borrowing fee risk premium is significantly different from zero, the magnitude of 0.31% is small. Thet-statistics for these hypothesis tests are based on robust standard errors double-clustered by stock and date. This approach takes into account any heteroskedasticity, contemporaneous cross-correlation, and autocorrelation exhibited by the error term.
In the subsample of hard-to-borrow stocks, defined as those with indicative fee greater than 1%, the mean and median values of the realized risk premium are 0.19% and 0.23% per year. Thet-statistic of 0.6 indicates that the estimate of the mean is not significantly different from zero for this important subsample. The mean of 0.19% is only 2% (= 0.19%/8.76%) of the mean adjusted option-implied borrowing fee of 8.76% per year. The subsample of hard-to-borrow stocks is the place where we would expect to find a substantial risk premium, if there is one, because constraints on supply are most likely to be binding for this subsample, possibly leading to loan recalls, higher and more volatile borrowing fees, and difficulties locating new shares to borrow when attempting to replace recalled stock loans. The fact that we do not find a substantial risk premium in this subsample is compelling evidence that the risk premium in the stock borrowing market is typically small. These results suggest that the implied fee can be used as a substitute for the actual borrowing fee when the latter is not available. For example, borrowing fees on a large cross section of stocks are only available starting from 2006, while option prices can be easily obtained back to 1996.
In TableIV, we verify that the potential for asynchronous end-of-day quotes in the CRSP and OptionMetrics data do not affect the main findings. We replicate the risk premium analysis in TableIIIusing the Nanex data set of intraday bid and ask option and equity quotes. For each stock and date, we compute the implied fee from the minute-by-minute synchronized option and stock quote midpoints for all valid put-call pairs, and use the median implied fee across the valid put-call pairs as the estimate of the implied fee for the day. We then use these daily implied fees to estimate the risk premium. The results in TableIVshow that the estimates of the risk premium based on intraday data are small in magnitude and not statistically significant at conventional levels for both the full sample and the subsample of hard-to-borrow stocks.
Figure2presents kernel regression estimates showing how the indicative fee, adjusted option-implied fee, and borrowing fee risk premium vary with utilization. The indicative fee and adjusted option-implied fee remain low for indicatives ranging from 0% to about 0.6%. The option-implied fee is slightly above the indicative fee for this range. Both variables increase rapidly as the indicative fee approaches 1% and the two fee measures are approximately linear in utilization once the indicative fee is above 1%. The estimate of the risk premium, however, does not follow the same pattern. Rather, it is approximately constant, becoming slightly negative when utilization is high. These results indicate that the 1% indicative fee cutoff used to construct the hard-to-borrow subsample is reasonable. The figure also supports our conclusion that the risk premium for hard-to-borrow stocks is modest and is not sensitive to this cutoff. Indeed, the estimated risk premium appears to be virtually zero even in extreme cases in which the indicative fee is very large.
Our measure of the risk premium assumes that the marginal market participant pays a stock borrowing fee equal to the Markit indicative borrowing fee to maintain any short positions. The fees paid by borrowers differ from the fees received by stock lenders due to prime broker markups. Since stock borrowing fees may be subject to negotiation between prime brokers and sophisticated market participants, such as options market makers, it is possible that sophisticated participants pay somewhat less than the indicative borrowing fee recorded by Markit.
While we do not observe the lender-side fees directly, we can adjust our estimate of the borrowing fee downward using the ratio of lender-side to borrower-side fees of 70% in D'Avolio (2002). If the sophisticated participants were to borrow at the midpoint of this 30% spread between borrower-side fees and lender-side fees, they would pay a fee 15% below the Markit indicative fee. The average fee from Markit is calculated as the difference between the average of the adjusted implied fee of 1.27% and the initial risk premium estimate of 0.31% in TableIII, or 0.96%, so the 15% savings is 0.15 × 0.96% = 0.14%. Adding this savings to the estimate of the risk premium, the estimate for the full sample would increase from 31 to 45 bps per year. Similar logic for the sample of stocks that are hard to borrow implies that the estimate of the risk premium would be slightly larger than 1% per year instead of a few basis points. This analysis likely provides an upper bound on the risk premium because it assumes that the marginal sophisticated participant can borrow at the midpoint of the spread between borrower- and lender-side fees, that is, the analysis assumes an unrealistically low cost of financial intermediation in the stock lending market.
One potential concern is that options’ bid-ask midpoints are not necessarily equal to their fair values. We compute the option-implied lending fee in equations (11) to (15) by equating the synthetic stock priceCt–Pt+PV(K) to the actual stock price less the present value of the borrowing fee (and dividends) as in the left-hand sides of equations (11) and (14). In the main analyses, we computeCtandPtusing bid-ask midpoints. Here, we instead bound the range of possible implied borrowing fees by using for each option pair the call ask price and put bid price, yielding a lower bound on the option-implied fee, or the call bid price and put ask price, yielding an upper bound on the option-implied fee. For each option pair, the implied fee spread is the difference between the implied fee based on the call bid and put ask prices and the implied fee based on the call ask and put bid prices. We compute the risk premium for a series of subsamples in which we impose successively tighter restrictions on this implied fee spread. For each stock-date, we compute the borrowing fee using the midpoint prices of the put-call pair corresponding to the minimum implied fee spread rather than the median implied fee across all valid put-call pairs. The specific restrictions on the options’ bid-ask spreads imposed by the limit on the implied fee spread depend on the options’ time to expiration (the number of remaining periodsk) through equations (14) to (15).
We report the risk premium estimates for this series of subsamples in TableV. The first row displays the results for both the full sample and the hard-to-borrow subsample without imposing a limit on the implied fee spread. The results in the first row of TableVare still different from those in TableIIIbecause the risk premium is calculated using the implied fee from the put-call pair with the minimum implied fee spread rather than the median implied fee across all valid put-call pairs. Consistent with the risk premium results in TableIII, the mean (median) risk premia are close to zero, −0.05% (−0.06%) and −0.37% (−0.27%), for both the full and hard-to-borrow samples, respectively.
The remaining rows of TableVreport results for subsamples selected by imposing successively tighter restrictions on the implied fee spread. For example, in the second row labeled “Implied fee spread minimum < 10%,” the risk premium estimates are computed from the put-call pair with the minimum implied fee spread using only the stock-dates for which the minimum implied fee spread is less than 10%. The results in the next three rows are based on the stock-dates for which the minimum implied fee spread is required to be less than 5%, less than 2%, and less than 1%, respectively. Both the mean and median estimates of the borrowing fee risk premium are close to zero for each of the subsamples. Thus, the finding that the borrowing fee risk premium is close to zero holds for subsamples in which deviations between the midpoint of the option quotes and fair value are unlikely to be relevant. This in turn suggests that the lack of a risk premium in the full sample is not likely to be driven by wide bid-ask spreads in the options used to compute the option-implied borrowing fees.
We present estimates of the borrowing fee risk premium for several other subsamples in TableVI.11The first row uses option pairs with time to maturity greater than 90 days to construct the implied fee. Note that this test uses a different subset of options than the other analyses that limit option maturity to between 15 and 90 days. Options with greater time to maturity are more likely to have lower relative bid-ask spreads, and the risk of a large change in the borrowing fee during the remaining life of the put-call pair is likely greater. The estimate of the risk premium is slightly negative using this alternative sample but still very close to zero.

& Date ≤ June 30, 2009

spread ≤ 1%
The options market maker exception to locate and close-out requirements, permitting widespread delivery failures by options market makers, was eliminated in September 2008. Prior to that time, options market makers were able to sell stock short without first borrowing the stock and then not deliver the shorted shares. Thus, our full-sample estimates of the risk premium for hard-to-borrow stocks may be impacted by the ability of options market makers to short stock and fail-to-deliver rather than borrow shares (Evans et al. (2009)). The evidence in Stratmann and Welborn (2013) as well as Jain and Jain (2015, Figure1) shows that the incidence of delivery failures for optionable stocks declined dramatically in September and October 2008.12Thus, if the absence of a substantial risk premium is due to the failure to deliver by options market makers, the risk premium should be larger after the rule change.13
We investigate this possibility by restricting the sample to observations from November 2008 or later and report the results in the second row of TableVI. We also restrict the sample to observations from 2013 or later.14SEC enforcement actions with respect to delivery failures began to take place in 2012, suggesting the alternative possibility that the extent to which shorting costs were reflected in option prices changed some time shortly before the start of 2013. The risk premium estimates for the sample period following November 1, 2008 as well as the sample period following January 1, 2013 are similar to the risk premium estimates for the full sample. For the hard-to-borrow stocks, the risk premium estimates for these subsamples are slightly smaller than their counterparts for the entire sample. Thus, the observed absence of a risk premium appears to be unrelated to option market maker exceptions that were relevant for part of the sample period.
We find some evidence of a substantial borrowing fee risk premium, particularly for hard-to-borrow stocks, in the fourth row of TableVI. The analysis in this row is restricted to observations from July 2008 to June 2009, the period closely associated with the financial crisis. Using all optionable stocks, the mean and median estimates of the risk premium are 0.77% and 0.46%, slightly larger than the full-sample estimates reported in TableIII. When we restrict the sample of observations to stocks with indicative borrowing greater than 1%, the average risk premium is 4.02% per year. This estimate is more than an order of magnitude larger than the corresponding 0.31% estimate of the risk premium for the entire sample.
There are two possible interpretations of this result. First, the average realized indicative borrowing fees during this period may have been less than expected, perhaps because the financial crisis was resolved more quickly than market participants expected. In this case, the realized risk premium may have exceeded its expectation. Alternatively, many aspects of financial intermediation became more exposed to systematic risk during these periods, and this may have resulted in volatile borrowing fees that carry a risk premium. Under this second interpretation, the financial crisis results help validate our estimates of the risk premium because we find evidence of a risk premium during a period when one was most likely. Regardless, the results for the financial crisis cannot explain the apparent abnormal performance of short strategies based on stock borrowing fees, short interest, or short fee risk documented in the literature because the presence of a substantial risk premium is limited to the short period associated with the financial crisis.
The remaining rows of TableVIrestrict the sample based on option characteristics to ensure that option prices are more likely to be large relative to options’ trading costs and that the observed option prices are not simply stale quotes. The fifth row restricts the sample to observations for which the sum of the call and put bid-ask spreads divided by the stock price is less than or equal to 1%. Any “noise” due to wide bid-ask spreads should be less relevant for this sample. The sixth row restricts the sample to observations for which the stocks have high average prices, and thus higher option prices and lower relative spreads. The seventh row restricts the sample to observations of put-call pairs with higher implied volatility and the eighth row restricts the sample to observations with higher average option volume during the previous month. With the exception of the financial crisis period, the largest estimate of the risk premium across all of the possible subsamples is 82 bps per year (or less than 7 bps per month). These estimates provide no evidence for an economically significant borrowing fee risk premium.
article.IV. Short Fee Risk and the Cross Section:
Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) argue that short-selling fee risk is relevant for the cross section of stock returns. They hypothesize that the high volatility of the borrowing fee prevents short sellers from taking sufficiently large short positions to drive down the current stock price immediately and eliminate the subsequent low returns. Their empirical results show that stocks with higher short fee risk, as measured by the estimates of fee volatility, tend to have lower subsequent stock returns, consistent with their prediction.
If this pattern is explained by a risk premium for exposure to short fee risk, then any risk premium should appear in the returns received by the short seller. The stocks with higher short-selling risk should therefore have lower subsequent stock returns, reflecting the risk premium that the short seller should earn for holding a short position. This prediction applies to the returns net of the stock borrowing fees that must be paid by the short seller to maintain the short position, the net-of-fee returns. Thus, an important implication of the Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) hypothesis is that the net-of-fee return to shorting should be predicted by short fee risk. We test this implication using panel regressions that examine whether short fee risk predicts both unadjusted stock returns and net-of-fee stock returns.
TableVIIpresents the results of these panel regressions for various specifications. We begin with univariate specifications that examine the ability of short fee risk to predict the unadjusted return, both in the full sample (column (1)) and in the subsample of stock-dates for which the stock is hard to borrow (column (6)). Thet-statistics are double-clustered by stock and date to account for any heteroskedasticity, contemporaneous cross-correlation, and autocorrelation potentially exhibited by the error term. We skip a day between the date when the covariates are observed and the beginning of the return interval, that is, we use information from datetto predict returns beginning from the close of trading on datet+ 1. This prevents the results from being impacted by the concerns identified by Goncalves-Pinto et al. (2020).
As expected given the results for unadjusted returns in Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018), the estimates in columns (1) and (6) confirm that there is a strong univariate relation between short fee risk, as measured by the volatility of the indicative borrowing fee during the previous 12 months, and subsequent stock returns.15The predictive relation between short fee risk and the subsequent return is negative and highly significant in both samples, witht-statistics of −4.3 and −3.8 in columns (1) and (6), respectively. The point estimate of the coefficient on short fee risk in the hard-to-borrow subsample is much larger than the estimate in the full sample. This pattern is not surprising because any proxy for short-sale costs or risks is likely to be a stronger predictor for hard-to-borrow stocks. This finding that short fee risk explains unadjusted returns is consistent with results reported by Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018). In fact, because short fee risk is correlated with other variables, such as the level of the lending fee and short interest, that are known to predict stock returns, this result would be expected even if the arguments about short fee risk in Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) were incorrect.
The specifications for which results are reported in columns (2) and (7) use the net-of-fee return as the dependent variable. The net-of-fee return is computed by cumulating the daily indicative fee over the 21-day return interval and subtracting it from the 21-day stock return, which is calculated using daily stock returns taken from CRSP. The results for the net-of-fee returns in columns (2) and (7) are strikingly different from those for the unadjusted returns in columns (1) and (6) the results in columns (2) and (7) provide no evidence of any relation between short fee risk and the net-of-fee return. Thet-statistics are only −0.7 and −0.4, respectively, and the point estimates of the coefficients are much smaller than the corresponding point estimates in columns (1) and (6). These results are inconsistent with the implications about the role of short fee risk in Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018). That is, we find no evidence of compensation for holding short positions exposed to short fee risk after taking into account the borrowing fees paid by short sellers to maintain the short positions.
The results for the hard-to-borrow subsample are particularly interesting because this subsample contains the set of observations for which predictability due to differences in the risk of changes in stock borrowing costs (short fee risk) may be most pronounced. To the extent that the cross section of subsequent stock returns is influenced by the limits to arbitrage created by limited supply of lendable shares in the market to borrow stock, these considerations should be more important at times when relatively high demand and or limited supply are more likely to generate borrowing constraints now or in the near future. Thus, the results show that short fee risk does not explain the cross section of returns in the subsample in which it should be the most relevant. The results in columns (5) and (10) confirm that the absence of a substantial relation between net-of-fee returns and short fee risk holds even if other measures of short-selling activity are included along with short fee risk in the regression specifications.
The specifications in columns (3), (4), (8), and (9) revisit using the unadjusted return as the dependent variable and include other characteristics of the stock borrowing market for each stock. The specifications in columns (3) and (8) include the option-implied and indicative borrowing fees and short interest, together with log market capitalization. The specifications in columns (4) and (9) additionally include utilization. Once these other covariates are included, short fee risk is no longer a statistically significant predictor of (even) unadjusted returns. In the full-sample results (columns (3) and (4)), the point estimates of the coefficient are close to zero and thet-statistics are only 0.1 and 0.9. Option-implied and indicative lending fees, short interest, and log market capitalization are significant in column (3), while in column (4) short interest becomes insignificant and utilization (which is highly correlated with short interest) is marginally significant. In the hard-to-borrow subsample (columns (8) and (9)), the coefficients on short fee risk are not statistically significant at conventional levels, witht-statistics of −1.6 and −1.5. In that subsample, the option-implied fee is the only significant predictor. These results show that short fee risk is not a robust predictor of unadjusted stock returns and is not a distinct predictor beyond measures of expected borrowing fees, such as the current indicative fee and the implied fee.
Since the implied borrowing fee does not exhibit a large risk premium, we interpret the implied borrowing fee as a proxy for the expected cost of holding a short position. The explanation of stock return predictability using the implied borrowing fee readily follows from this interpretation. Essentially, the current and expected future borrowing fee, rather than the risk properties of the borrowing fee, is the important limit to arbitrage. This explanation is consistent with the idea that short sellers should be less likely to take large short positions when shorting is expensive, for example, Miller (1977). We generalize this concept because the expected cost of the short position during the subsequent week, month, or quarter, rather than only the current cost of the short position, constitutes the limit to arbitrage.
article.V. Additional Analyses:
In this section, we report results for several additional analyses that either validate our computation and interpretation of the option-implied borrowing fee or support the main analyses described above. These analyses include estimating the borrowing fee risk premium for easy-to-borrow stocks and examining whether the option-implied fee predicts changes in the indicative fee. We also examine changes in the borrowing fee risk premium around the 2008 short-sale ban.
We expect the risk premium to be small for the subsample of observations for which the stock is easy to borrow, and even smaller for the subsample for which the stock is easy to borrow and short fee risk is low. The results reported in TableVIIIsupport this conjecture. The left-hand side of the table presents estimates of the risk premium and the implied and indicative fees for the subsample of stock-dates for which the stock was easy to borrow, where a stock is considered easy to borrow if the indicative borrowing fee is less than or equal to 1%. The right-hand side of TableVIIIdisplays the estimates for a subsample of stock-dates for which the stock is easy to borrow and short fee risk is below the median. The results in the first line of the table show that the mean and median estimates of the risk premium are indeed close to zero: the means (medians) in the easy-to-borrow and low short fee risk subsamples are 0.33% (0.28%) and 0.22% (0.23%), respectively. This evidence confirms that the choices made when translating option prices into implied borrowing fees, for example, the way we handle the possible early exercise of options, do not have an important effect on our estimates of the risk premium. If they did, we would not expect to recover such small risk premiums for these subsamples. The results also indicate that options’ wide bid-ask spreads do not prevent us from accurately estimating the borrowing fee risk premium.
We estimate the borrowing fee risk premium as the average difference between the option-implied borrowing fee and the accumulated indicative fee over the lives of the options and find that the risk premium is small. This result is consistent with the interpretation that the option-implied fee provides an estimate of current and expected future stock borrowing costs. If the implied fee contains information about expected future stock borrowing costs, then it should help predict subsequent changes in the indicative borrowing fee.16
To test this prediction, we regress the change in the borrowing fee during the subsequent month on the current indicative fee, the implied borrowing fee, several other covariates that are proxies for short-sale constraints or measures of short-selling activity, and the log market capitalization of the stock. The regression models are estimated using the sample of all stock-dates as well as the hard-to-borrow subsample, where a stock is considered hard to borrow on a date if its indicative fee is greater than 1%.
We report the results in TableIX. The results in the first four columns correspond to the full sample, while those in the last four columns correspond to the hard-to-borrow subsample. The coefficient on the indicative fee is negative for the full-sample specification that only includes the indicative fee (column (1)), consistent with the indicative fee following a mean-reverting process. For the specification that includes both option-implied and indicative fees (column (2)), both variables are significant predictors of the change in the borrowing fee. The coefficient on the implied borrowing fee is positive, as anticipated, and highly significant (t-statistic = 9.4). The coefficient on the indicative fee is negative, again consistent with mean-reversion. The results in column (3) show that short fee risk and short interest are also significant predictors of the change in the borrowing fee, as is market capitalization. However, including these variables does not impact the result that the implied and indicative fees both predict the change in the indicative fee the estimated coefficients on the implied and indicative fees in column (3) are similar in magnitude to those in column (2). The significant coefficient on short fee risk is not a robust result, as this coefficient is small and insignificant (t-statistic =  0.1) once utilization is included in the specification in column (4). In column (4), the estimated coefficients on the implied and indicative fees are again similar to those in column (2).
Columns (5) to (8) report the results of the same four specifications estimated using data from the subsample of stock dates for which the stock is hard to borrow. For all four specifications, the estimated coefficients on the implied fee are much larger than those in the regression models estimated on the full sample. This is to be expected because the option-implied borrowing fee is larger relative to any errors in the estimates of it for this subsample, and because hard-to-borrow stocks tend to have higher and more variable borrowing fees. Except for short fee risk, the estimated coefficients on the additional covariates included in the specifications reported in columns (7) and (8) are also larger than the corresponding coefficients in columns (3) and (4). This is also unsurprising because cross-sectional differences in the subsequent change in the borrowing fee are likely to be more tightly related to various measures of short-sale constraints in this subsample because such constraints are clearly present for hard-to-borrow stocks. Short fee risk is not a significant predictor of changes in fees in columns (7) to (8), again indicating that the significant coefficient on it in column (3) is not a robust result.
Overall, the results reported in TableIXsupport the hypothesis that the option-implied borrowing fee reflects current and expected future stock borrowing costs for the underlying stock. This finding also explains why the implied fee may be quite different from the current indicative fee even in the absence of a risk premium or substantial estimation error.
In the early morning hours of September 19, 2008, the SEC announced a ban on short sales of approximately 800 financial stocks and subsequently increased the list of banned stocks to almost 1,000. The short-sale ban ended on October 9, 2008.17During the periods shortly before and after the ban, options market makers were plausibly worried about the possible imposition or reimposition of a policy banning the short-selling of financial stocks from which they might not be exempt. The ban prohibited opening new short positions in most financial stocks but allowed existing short positions to remain open. We exclude the period of the short-sale ban itself because as Grundy, Lim, and Verwijmeren (2012) and Battalio and Schultz (2011) show, option bid-ask spreads exploded during the ban, preventing us from computing accurate estimates of option-implied fees.
In TableX, we present estimates of borrowing fee risk premiums for several periods before and after the short-sale ban. The two columns under the heading “Indicative Fee” report the average indicative fee for the stocks that were banned and those that were not banned for the several periods. While the average indicative fees for the banned stocks are slightly less than those for the stocks that were not banned, the differences are not economically important. In addition, indicative fees remained stable near 1% around the ban for all stocks.
The next two columns compare the average borrowing fee risk premium for the banned versus not-banned stocks. The point estimates in these two columns suggest that the risk premium was elevated in the two-month period just prior to and just after the ban on shorting financial stocks. The columns headed “Difference and Diff. (w/o controls)” examine whether the elevated risk premiums during this period are statistically significant. The coefficient estimates andt-statistics in the columns headed “Difference” are from specifications with an indicator variable equal to one for banned financial stocks and zero for other stocks in panel regressions that explain the risk premia, controlling for market capitalization, stock return volatility (estimated as the idiosyncratic volatility from a three-factor Fama-French model over the previous 21 days), and the concurrent stock return.18The coefficient estimate on the indicator variable was 0.59% (0.95%) during the two-month period before (after) the ban, with at-statistic of 2.5 (3.2). The results in the columns headed “Diff. (w/o controls)” correspond to panel regressions without the controls and show similar patterns. We find very similar results if we use an alternative ex ante risk premium equal to the difference between the current option-implied fee and the indicative borrowing fee. Thus, we find evidence consistent with a small though statistically significant increase in the borrowing fee risk premium for financial stocks around the short-sale ban.
However, the period around the short-sale ban was relatively short, and it may be the case that the average realized borrowing fee during this period was less than expected. Thus, the realized risk premium may simply have exceeded its expectation for this period. Nevertheless, our ability to detect modest changes in the risk premium around the short-sale ban indicates that our finding that the risk premium is near zero for most stocks in most time periods is likely not due to a flawed statistical test with limited power.
article.VI. Conclusion:
Measures of short-selling activity and short-sale constraints that are readily available to sophisticated investors predict stock returns. This finding, documented extensively in the literature, presents a puzzle. Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) propose a resolution of the puzzle. They argue that short fee risk is an impediment to arbitrage that limits the ability of arbitrageurs to exploit the predictability, allowing it to persist.
An implication of the discussion in Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) is that short sellers should be willing to pay a risk premium to avoid the risks of fee changes and loan recalls. If there were a market for term stock borrowing, the borrowing fee risk premium would be measured by the difference between the term borrowing fee and the expectation of the accumulated fee during the life of the term loan. While there is no active market for term stock loans, the term borrowing fee can be estimated by the option-implied borrowing fee. The arguments in Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) imply that this option-implied fee should reflect a risk premium, which can be estimated as the average difference between the option-implied fee and the accumulated borrowing fee during the life of the option put-call pair used to compute the option-implied fee.
We conduct this exercise and find that the estimates of the risk premium are small. This is inconsistent with a central implication of Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018), as it indicates that the risk of borrowing stock does not lead to a substantial borrowing fee risk premium. Instead, the results are consistent with the view that option-implied borrowing fees reflect the expected cost of borrowing stock during the lives of the options used to compute the option-implied fees. Since option prices are readily available to all investors, these results also indicate that one valid measure of the expected cost of short-selling can be computed directly from public information.
A second implication of Engelberg, Reed, and Ringgenberg (2018) is that the returns to short-selling net of borrowing fees should also reflect a premium to compensate short sellers for bearing short fee risk. This implies that the net-of-fee return to shorting should be predicted by short fee risk. We find that it is not. In contrast, the option-implied borrowing fee predicts future stock returns even after controlling for other measures of short-selling activity, consistent with its interpretation as a measure of the current and expected future costs of borrowing stock.
Additional analyses validate our computation and interpretation of the option-implied fee and support the main analyses. In particular, the risk premium estimate near zero for the subsample of easy-to-borrow stocks with low short fee risk indicates that our computation of the implied fee adjusted for early exercise is reasonable.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
